metadata.title: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The formula lambda equals h over p gave a walking person ten to the minus thirty-five meters, twenty orders below a proton, so no slit exists to diffract me. I want to find where the crossover actually lives. Please: (1) show what momentum p would push a human wavelength up to a one-millimeter slit, (2) redo the buckyball number to confirm its 2.5 picometer wavelength beats its own size, and (3) explain what physical property sets the largest object that can still diffract today.

props.command: The formula lambda equals h over p gave a walking person ten to the minus thirty-five meters, twenty orders below a proton, so no slit exists to diffract me. I want to find where the crossover actually lives. Please: (1) show what momentum p would push a human wavelength up to a one-millimeter slit, (2) redo the buckyball number to confirm its 2.5 picometer wavelength beats its own size, and (3) explain what physical property sets the largest object that can still diffract today.
props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through

beats.narration_text: Electrons diffract through crystals. Buckyballs make interference fringes. You walk through a doorway every day and nothing happens. Quantum mechanics applies to you. So why don't you diffract?

props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
props.command: what is my de Broglie wavelength?

props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: de Broglie formula lambda = h/p — show h = 6.626e-34 Js, illustrate that the same formula applies to electrons and people"

beats.narration_text: De Broglie said: a particle with momentum p has wavelength h over p. The same formula that describes an electron also describes a person — the only question is what number comes out.
An electron at fifty-four electron-volts has wavelength 0.167 nanometers — comparable to atom spacing. Diffraction happens. Why not for you?

card.copy:
Electrons: lambda = 0.167 nm — atom-sized. Diffract.
You at 1 m/s: lambda = ?
Why does the same formula not give you fringes?

props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: human de Broglie wavelength calculation — 70 kg person at 1 m/s, momentum = 70 kg m/s, lambda = 6.626e-34 / 70 approximately 9.5e-36 m"

beats.narration_text: Plug in the numbers. A seventy kilogram person walking at one meter per second has momentum seventy kilogram-meters-per-second. De Broglie wavelength: six point six times ten to the minus thirty-four, divided by seventy — that is ten to the minus thirty-five meters.

props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: logarithmic scale comparison — human wavelength 1e-35 m vs proton radius 1e-15 m vs atom 1e-10 m vs electron wavelength 1.67e-10 m"

beats.narration_text: Ten to the minus thirty-five meters. For comparison, a proton has radius ten to the minus fifteen meters. Your quantum wavelength is twenty orders of magnitude smaller than a proton. No slit, grating, or crystal anywhere in the universe has spacings this small.

props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: diffraction condition lambda approximately equal to gap size — electron fits through atom-sized slit, human needs 1e-35 m slit 20 orders of magnitude smaller than any particle"

beats.narration_text: For diffraction to appear, the wavelength must be comparable to the gap size. An electron fits through an atom-sized slit. You would need a slit as narrow as ten to the minus thirty-five meters — twenty times smaller than any known particle.

props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: quantum mechanics never turns off — wave function of a person satisfies Schrodinger, interference fringes exist at 1e-35 m period, undetectable but real"

beats.narration_text: Quantum mechanics is not absent for you — it is present but the fringes are compressed into a spacing too fine to resolve. The wave function of a person walking exists and satisfies Schrodinger's equation. Its interference pattern has a fringe period of ten to the minus thirty-five meters.

props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: buckyball C60 diffraction — wavelength approximately 2.5 pm, Zeilinger 1999 experiment with 50 nm slits, fringes at the edge of measurability"

beats.narration_text: Large molecules do diffract — just barely. A carbon-sixty buckyball has wavelength about two point five picometers, smaller than the molecule itself. Zeilinger's Vienna group made it diffract through a grating in 1999. The fringes were real, but the slit had to be only fifty nanometers wide.

props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: classical limit — as mass and speed increase, de Broglie wavelength shrinks below any grating resolution, quantum never turns off but becomes invisible"

beats.narration_text: The classical world is not a different regime of physics. It is the limit where the wavelength is so small that no available grating can reveal the wave structure. Quantum mechanics never stops applying — it just becomes undetectable as mass and speed grow.

Same formula for everything. Electron wavelength: atomic scale, diffraction visible. Human wavelength: ten to the minus thirty-five, no grating exists. Quantum mechanics never turns off — the fringes just become too fine to see.

card.copy:
lambda = h / p for everything.
Electron: 0.167 nm — diffraction visible.
Person: 1e-35 m — no grating exists.
Quantum never turns off.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Four objects, one formula. Ask Claude to calculate and compare wavelengths across twenty orders of magnitude. See exactly how far you are from the quantum world.

props.segment: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
props.command: calculate the de Broglie wavelength for: an electron at 54 eV, a proton at thermal speed at 300K, a 70 kg person at 1 m/s, and a 100-ton airplane at cruising speed — compare all four on a single logarithmic scale diagram

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Same formula for everything. Electron wavelength: atomic scale, diffraction visible. Human wavelength: ten to the minus thirty-five, no grating exists. Quantum mechanics never turns off — the fringes just become too fine to see. Your turn. Four objects, one formula. Ask Claude to calculate and compare wavelengths across twenty orders of magnitude. See exactly how far you are from the quantum world.

props.artifactHeading: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
artifactLines: The classical world is not a different regime of physics.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
